explanations.1: Angular acceleration is the change in angular velocity divided by time (10 rad/s / 5 s = 2 rad/s²).
explanations.2: Tangential acceleration affects the linear speed of a point and is calculated as the product of the radius and angular acceleration.
explanations.3: Tangential acceleration is found using the formula $a_t = r\alpha$ (0.5 m × 4.0 rad/s² = 2.0 m/s²).
explanations.4: Physics conventions define counter-clockwise as the positive rotational direction and clockwise as negative.
explanations.5: Using the kinematic equation $\theta = \omega_0t + 0.5\alpha t^2$, we get $0 + 0.5(2.0)(5^2) = 25$ rad.
explanations.6: The angle $\theta$ describes the change in position in rotational motion, just as $x$ describes it in linear motion.
explanations.7: Average angular velocity for constant acceleration is the mean of the initial and final velocities (600 + 0)/2 = 300 rpm.
explanations.8: The linear distance is related to the angle by $x = r\theta$, so $0.20 \times 10 = 2.0$ meters.
explanations.9: Torque is the "turning effectiveness" of a force and causes angular acceleration, similar to how force causes linear acceleration.
explanations.10: By definition, the moment of inertia for a point mass is the mass multiplied by the square of its distance from the pivot.
explanations.11: Torque depends on the perpendicular distance from the pivot; a longer lever arm increases the turning effect.
explanations.12: According to the rotational analog of Newton's second law ($\alpha = \tau/I$), angular acceleration is directly proportional to torque.
explanations.13: Energy is a scalar quantity and is measured in Joules (J) regardless of whether it is translational or rotational.
explanations.14: The disk has a smaller moment of inertia, meaning less energy goes into rotation and more goes into translation (speed).
explanations.15: Rotational kinetic energy is calculated as $KE_{rot} = 0.5I\omega^2$ ($0.5 \times 4.0 \times 10^2 = 200$ J).
explanations.16: Because thick soup sticks to the can and rotates, more of the potential energy is converted to rotational energy, leaving less for forward speed.
explanations.17: Angular momentum is $L = I\omega$, which is the rotational analog to linear momentum $p = mv$.
explanations.18: In the absence of external torque, the total angular momentum of a system is conserved.
explanations.19: Since $L = I\omega$ is constant, if $I$ decreases by a factor of 3, $\omega$ must increase by a factor of 3 to compensate.
explanations.20: The rate of change of angular momentum is equal to the net external torque applied to the system.
explanations.21: External forces at the pivot do not change the angular momentum because they act at the axis of rotation ($r = 0$).
explanations.22: The percussion point is the specific location where the impact produces no reaction force at the pivot (the hand).
explanations.23: In inelastic collisions, kinetic energy is not conserved, though total energy and angular momentum are.
explanations.24: The right-hand rule is the convention used to define the direction of the angular velocity and angular momentum vectors along the axis of rotation.
explanations.25: The Sun and Moon exert a gravitational torque on Earth's equatorial bulge, causing it to act like a precessing gyroscope.
explanations.26: A perpendicular torque changes the direction of the angular momentum vector without necessarily changing its magnitude.
explanations.27: A hoop has all its mass at the maximum distance $R$ from the axis, giving it the maximum possible $I = MR^2$.
explanations.28: Using $mgh = PE$, we have $1000 \times 9.8 \times h = 4.9 \times 10^5$, which solves to $h = 50$ meters.
explanations.29: As the radius of the rotating air decreases, its moment of inertia decreases, requiring an increase in angular velocity to keep $L$ constant.
explanations.30: 60 rpm is 1 rev/s, and since there are $2\pi$ radians in one revolution, the velocity is $2\pi$ rad/s.
